data.title: Chinese state media repositions sci-fi space and air combat aircraft as tech target
data.description:
Integrated space-air fighters featured in China’s Nantianmen science-fiction project, represent the future of aerospace technology, and it is only a matter of time before they are realised, according to state media.
The comments appeared on Friday in a programme on CCTV’s military channel that repositions the Nantianmen Project – a research initiative dating back to 2017 that focused on future air and space warfare – from a collection of fantasy concepts to attainable future tech.
In the programme, CCTV showcased conceptual fighter jets that had previously been unveiled along with fresh animations to show them in space combat.


Conceptual models from the project – including the Baidi aerospace fighter – have been displayed at domestic air shows since the project was launched.
Until last week’s CCTV programme, observers had generally regarded the models as science fiction-inspired models produced by China’s aerospace industry, primarily for educational and public engagement purposes, but a long way from operational reality.
However, in Friday’s broadcast, military analyst Wang Mingzhi, of the PLA Air Force Command College, said, “these frontier technologies [from the Nantianmen Project] reflect both expectations for future aerospace and space superiority, and the directions being pursued to safeguard national security”.
Wang said the project integrated a range of advanced technologies into futuristic combat aircraft. Those technologies included hypersonic flight, dual-mode air-and-space propulsion, metamaterial stealth, adaptive airframe configurations, autonomous unmanned swarm coordination, AI-enabled decision making, directed-energy weapons and space-to-atmosphere operations.
“These frontier technologies are not a question of whether they can be achieved,” Wang said, “but rather which ones will be realised first, and when will they ultimately all be realised.”
The project began in 2017 as a futuristic aviation-themed project centred around designing a global integrated defence system, with large aerospace carrier platforms and fighters and tactical mechanised assets, according to Chinese media reports.

It was initially developed by AVIC Global Culture Communication (Beijing) Company, a subsidiary of the Aviation Industry Corporation of China.
In 2018, a concept model of the aerospace carrier Luanniao was first shown to the public at the Zhuhai air show in Guangdong province.
According to the January 9 CCTV programme, the 100,000-tonne Luanniao was designed with an overall length of 242 metres (794 feet) and a wingspan of 684 metres.
It was envisioned to carry up to 88 unmanned Xuannv fighters, concept aircraft described as highly manoeuvrable, stealthy and intelligent. The aircraft were imagined to operate within the atmosphere and outer space and would be equipped with particle acceleration cannons and hypersonic missiles.
The CCTV programme also animated the Baidi fighter, or White Emperor, showing it flying with two drones while engaging in a fictional encounter with an alien aircraft.
In conceptual planning, the Baidi is positioned as the primary combat platform within the Nantianmen Project and is likened to a “transforming” aircraft because of its stealth shaping and complex variable-geometry wing structures.
A life-size model of the Baidi was on show at the 2024 Zhuhai air show. CCTV said that model reflected upgrades from previous versions, including changes to the tail configuration and expanded internal weapons bays for increased payload capacity.
Improvements to its avionics systems better supported operations in near-space environments, the broadcaster said.
The Baidi design incorporates full-spectrum stealth, optional manned or unmanned operation, unmanned aircraft command capabilities and adaptive engines. Its modular payloads allowed rapid switching between mission roles, while the variable-wing structure dynamically adjusted aerodynamic configuration according to altitude, speed and flight attitude, the report said.


The programme also featured another concept aircraft, the Zihuo, or Purple Fire, vertical take-off and landing (VTOL) platform, which debuted at a Tianjin air show last year.
It is designed for both manned and unmanned use, with a projected cruising speed of 700–800km/h and the ability to operate in low-gravity or environments with a thin atmosphere.
It was envisioned to support autonomous and formation flight, as well as missions such as rescue operations and logistics delivery in high-risk areas.

data.title: Cuba’s Diaz say ‘no conversations’ with US after Trump threats amid Venezuela tensions
data.description:
Cuba’s leader said on Monday there were “no conversations” with the United States, after US President Donald Trump urged the communist-ruled island to “make a deal” or pay the price.
“There are no conversations with the US government except for technical contacts in the area of migration,” Miguel Diaz-Canel wrote on social media after Trump said the nations were in talks following the US military toppling of Havana’s leftist ally Nicolas Maduro of Venezuela.
Díaz-Canel wrote that for “relations between the US and Cuba to progress, they must be based on international law rather than hostility, threats, and economic coercion.”
He added: “We have always been willing to hold a serious and responsible dialogue with the various US governments, including the current one, on the basis of sovereign equality, mutual respect, principles of international law, and mutual benefit without interference in internal affairs and with full respect for our independence.”

The US president told ‍reporters on Sunday that the US was “talking to ‍Cuba.” He did not specify what had been discussed in ‌the alleged conversations but said “you´ll find out pretty ‍soon.”
Washington has imposed economy-crippling sanctions on its island neighbour for decades, but Trump has ramped up the pressure in recent days.
“THERE WILL BE NO MORE OIL OR MONEY GOING TO CUBA – ZERO!” Trump said on his Truth Social platform. “I strongly suggest they make a deal, BEFORE IT IS TOO LATE.”
Trump provided almost no details about what potential deal he was referring to.
On Sunday, Trump wrote that Cuba would no longer live off oil and money from Venezuela, which the US attacked on January 3 in a stunning operation that killed 32 Cuban officers and led to the arrest of President Nicolas Maduro.




Cuba was receiving an estimated 35,000 barrels a day from Venezuela before the US attacked, along with some 5,500 barrels daily from Mexico and roughly 7,500 from Russia, according to Jorge Pinon of the Energy Institute at the University of Texas at Austin, who tracks the shipments.
The situation between the US and Cuba is “very sad and concerning,” said Andy S. Gomez, retired dean of the School of International Studies and senior fellow in Cuban Studies at University of Miami.
He said he sees Diaz-Canel’s latest comments “as a way to try and buy a little bit of time for the inner circle to decide what steps it’s going to take.”
Gomez said he doesn’t visualise Cuba reaching out to US officials right now.
“They had every opportunity when President Obama opened up US diplomatic relations, and yet they didn’t even bring Cuban coffee to the table,” Gomez said. “Of course, these are desperate times for Cuba.”
The island’s communist government has said US sanctions cost the country more than US$7.5 billion between March 2024 and February 2025.

data.title: NBA targets US$1 billion team valuations as it courts investors for Europe league
data.description:
The National Basketball Association has prepared a pitch to investors on a new European league, targeting team valuations of up to US$1 billion, as it readied a pair of regular season games in London and Berlin.
NBA commissioner Adam Silver will attend a pair of regular season games in London and Berlin starting on Thursday between the Memphis Grizzlies and the Orlando Magic. Both cities are likely to host a team in a league that could debut as early as next year, according to sources.
The NBA will also open its data room for the planned European league to investors in the coming days, including financial projections, the sources said. The project would include 12 franchises and four additional teams that qualify for the competition without owning equity, while the NBA would retain a 50 per cent stake in the new league.
Fiba, the world governing body for basketball, would have a stake in the new league, along with the team owners, one of the people said.
The NBA would also hold a private conference in London to meet potential sponsors, media partners and investors in mid-January, according to people familiar with the situation. Existing NBA owners have also expressed interest in buying stakes in European teams and the league, the people added. NBA rules currently prohibit the ownership of more than one team.

“We’re casting a very, very wide net right now and essentially saying to anyone who’s interested, come see our bankers,” Silver said in December. “Explain to us why you’re interested, how you view the opportunity, what resources you would put behind opening a team.”
The lofty valuations could place the teams among the most expensive sporting assets in Europe, topped only by around 24 football clubs and Formula One teams, even though basketball has a much smaller audience than those sports on the continent. The NBA’s strong brand and Europe’s existing infrastructure could help create a popular league based in its major cities, the league has argued.
The NBA might not require investors to commit large amounts of cash up front and the league was considering allowing them to buy in over a number of years, the people said.
Breaking into Europe has become one of Silver’s flagship goals during his tenure. There has been at least one regular season game in Paris in each of the past three seasons. In March last year, Silver held a press conference with Andreas Zagklis, the secretary general of Fiba, where they outlined a vision for a new league.
The NBA has held early-stage discussions with a number of football club owners, including Paris Saint-Germain and Manchester City, about potential investment, Bloomberg reported earlier.
Basketball is popular in Europe, especially in Greece, Spain and the Balkans. A record 71 European players were on NBA rosters at the beginning of the current season, or about one in six players, including three-time MVP Nikola Jokic, two-time MVP Giannis Antetokounmpo and All-Star Victor Wembanyama.

A potential obstacle is EuroLeague, the continent’s 20-team incumbent that is the highest-level basketball league in the world after the NBA. The pan-European competition features Europe’s biggest basketball brands, including Real Madrid and Barcelona.
EuroLeague extended its partnership with global sports marketing firm IMG last year, giving the agency media rights distribution for the next decade, in a sign that most of the club-owned league’s shareholders back the status quo.
The NBA could face political resistance in Europe. Countries including Italy and France spoke out against the NBA’s plans at a November meeting of European Union sports ministers in Brussels, Politico reported last month.
Nine of EuroLeague’s 13 permanent clubs have extended their licences for 10 years starting next season.
“The remaining clubs have not communicated any decisions yet, but we are confident that they will commit,” EuroLeague Basketball chief executive officer Paulius Motiejunas said. “These teams will want to be owners of the bright future that awaits the EuroLeague rather than becoming someone else’s franchise.”

data.title: Japan firms seen ‘stockpiling’ rare earths as Tokyo looks to G7 in face of China controls
data.description:
Fears of China choking off exports of critical minerals to Japan amid a deepening political dispute have set off industry alarms and prompted Tokyo to elevate the issue at a G7 gathering of finance ministers this week, despite Beijing’s assurances that civilian trade would be spared.
Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said last week that she would attend the Group of Seven event in Washington on Monday with the “risk of a rare-earth-supply interruption from China in mind”, the Tokyo-based Jiji Press reported. Officials from Canada, the United States and Australia were also expected to participate.
The inclusion of rare earths on the G7 agenda reflects heightened concern in Japan over China controlling exports of 17 rare earth elements that are crucial for Japan’s massive manufacturing sector, from consumer electronics to vehicles, according to analysts.
Although Japan has reduced its reliance on Chinese rare earths since 2010, when China stopped rare earth exports for two months following a vessel collision near disputed islets, it still depends heavily on Chinese supplies, as replacements take time, the experts said.
“They have an immediate problem, and they need to find sources,” said Rajiv Biswas, CEO of Singapore-based research firm Asia-Pacific Economics.
Analysts say businesses remain on edge because China processes about 90 per cent of the world’s rare earths, as estimated by the Centre for Strategic and International Studies think tank, and sells them at relatively low prices.
“There are supply chain routes not going through China, but the problem is that the cost is much, much higher,” said Kazuto Suzuki, a professor of science and technology policy with the Graduate School of Public Policy at the University of Tokyo. “They worry that, if it goes on, they will not get access to the cheap rare earths. It will be licensed but will take a long time.”


Beijing announced last week that it would control exports to Japan of “dual-use” items – products with both commercial and military applications – but the state-run Xinhua said China’s measures would “curb Japan’s remilitarisation” rather than cause concern for “civilian trade”.
Suzuki said the items may include rare earths.
Controls usually mean that a Chinese exporter must apply to the government for permission to make specific shipments, which are subject to approval based on the buyer’s identity.
China is unofficially “choking off” exports of rare earths and rare earth magnets already, The Wall Street Journal reported on Thursday, citing Japanese media.
Asked on Monday by a Japanese journalist about reports that Chinese companies had notified some Japanese buyers that they would not sign new rare earth contracts, Ministry of Foreign Affairs spokeswoman Mao Ning said at a regular news conference in Beijing that China had “taken relevant measures in accordance with laws and regulations”.
The dual-use restrictions, along with a November travel advisory against Japan and a ban on Japanese seafood imports, follow Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s November 7 remarks about Taiwan, which angered the Chinese government.
The G7 finance ministers – representatives of the world’s most advanced economies – said last month that they had “committed to work together to diversify and de-risk supply chains” affecting critical minerals, a category that includes rare earths. France, Germany, Italy and the United Kingdom also belong to the group.


Biswas noted that Japan might use this week’s G7 event to kick-start bilateral or multiparty agreements on sourcing rare earths from nations such as Australia.
For its part, the US reacted to China’s early 2025 controls over rare earth exports targeting American buyers by investing in domestic mining and rights to overseas minerals.
Many in Japan have suggested that a Chinese rare earth “ban” is imminent, and Tokyo is taking the matter seriously because of the 2010 precedent, said Jeffrey Kingston, a professor specialising in Asian studies and history at Temple University, Japan Campus.
Japan’s dependence on Chinese rare earths fell from 90 per cent in 2010 to 60 per cent by October 2023, according to the World Economic Forum.
Analysts attributed that drop to imports from other countries, localised mineral production and the use of non-rare earths for Japan’s domestic industry, but shifting away from China has been a slow process.
“Japanese executives, most of them, remember what happened in 2010,” Suzuki said.
Kingston estimated that the Japanese government has spent US$15 billion to reduce reliance on Chinese rare earths since 2010. But industry players, he said, “are very vulnerable and stockpiling for the next rainy day”.
Japan is getting “prepared” for a scenario in which stocks run out, said Andy Xie, an independent economist in Shanghai. He added that the preparations signal “they are not going to back down” over the political dispute.

data.title: China’s fight against corruption is a battle we can’t afford to lose, Xi Jinping warns
data.description:
Chinese President Xi Jinping has said there must be no room for corrupt elements to hide as he warned that the problem was a threat to the country’s development.
On Monday, he told a plenary session of the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection (CCDI) – China’s top anti-corruption body – that the country had made notable progress last year, but the issue remained a “major struggle”.
“Corruption is a stumbling block and obstacle to the development of the [Communist] party and the country, and the fight against corruption is a major struggle that we cannot afford to lose,” he said, according to state news agency Xinhua.
“Currently, the situation in the fight against corruption remains grave and complex … We must maintain a high-pressure stance without wavering, resolutely punishing corruption wherever it exists, eliminating all forms of graft, and leaving no place for corrupt elements to hide.”
Xi also urged officials to come up with new methods to discover, identify and tackle all types of corruption.
This year will see a major overhaul of local governments across the country, and Xi said the party “must deploy cadres who are truly loyal, reliable, consistent and responsible”.
Xi launched a major crackdown on corruption when he came to power more than a decade ago. Last year saw a record 65 senior officials detained by the CCDI, up from 58 in 2024.


He also said China needed to “push forward its fight against corruption with a clearer understanding and stronger resolve”, adding that these efforts would provide a “strong guarantee” that the goals of the latest five-year plan for the country’s development would be achieved.
Xi described the next five years as a “crucial period for laying a solid foundation and making comprehensive efforts to achieve socialist modernisation”.
China’s 15th five-year plan, set to be approved in March, maps the key priorities for the country’s economy, technology and defence from 2026 to 2030, a period seen as pivotal for Beijing in its deepening rivalry with the United States.
China faces multiple challenges both at home and abroad, including Western restrictions on technology, an ageing population, local debt and weak consumer confidence.
Xi added, “We must uphold the principle that everyone is equal before the law … and that there are no exceptions in enforcing laws and regulations.”

data.title: Jimmy Lai trial: Hong Kong lawyers for publisher, activists seek reduced jail time
data.description:
Lawyers for a former newspaper publisher and two activists have called for less than 10 years’ imprisonment for their roles in attempts to instigate sanctions against Hong Kong and mainland China, saying they deserve additional reductions in securing Jimmy Lai Chee-ying’s conviction in his national security trial.
Arguments over appropriate sentences for the former media boss and eight other defendants began on Monday at West Kowloon Court. Aside from Lai, the rest pleaded guilty before his conviction for conspiring to collude with foreign forces.
Apple Daily publisher Cheung Kim-hung, associate publisher Chan Pui-man, editor-in-chief Ryan Law Wai-kwong, executive editor-in-chief Lam Man-chung, managing editor Fung Wai-kong and editorial writer Yeung Ching-kee admitted using the now-defunct tabloid-style newspaper to instigate hatred against authorities and attract international sanctions before its closure in June 2021.
Wayland Chan Tsz-wah and Andy Li Yu-hin, core members of the “Fight for Freedom, Stand with Hong Kong” (SWHK) lobbying group, pleaded guilty to spreading propaganda and influencing foreign policy on China to trigger hostile actions from foreign governments.
Lucas Lau Cheuk-yin, Cheung’s counsel, highlighted his client’s donations to charitable causes, saying he had given more than HK$5 million (US$641,300) to the Apple Daily Charitable Foundation for the benefit of cancer patients and underprivileged children.
Lau urged the court to reduce Cheung’s jail sentence to less than five years, arguing that his offence was less serious than what prosecutors alleged.
The publisher, a prosecution witness in his former boss’s trial, had sought to reduce the harm caused by his actions, such as by removing offensive articles from Apple Daily’s online platforms, the lawyer said.
Mr Justice Alex Lee Wan-tang, however, said it would be “unrealistic” not to consider the present case as one “of a grave nature”, which would attract a minimum jail term of 10 years under the national security law.
Priscilia Lam Tsz-ying SC, for Wayland Chan, said her client’s sentence should be reduced to less than 10 years to reflect the “significant” risk he had taken to help prosecutors secure Lai’s conviction.
Lam revealed that Chan’s family had suffered doxxing attacks since his prosecution in 2021, with people sharing different political views making “unkind” remarks in repeated phone calls to his parents about his decision to testify against Lai.
Chan said he feared going abroad after his release from prison due to possible retaliation against his actions after his arrest.
He is currently receiving psychological counselling, taking business management courses and pursuing religious studies with a view to reintegrating into society in the future.
Alain Sham Chung-ping, Li’s counsel, urged the court to adopt a 10-year starting point and reduce his client’s sentence by half for his assistance rendered in Lai’s prosecution.
Sham said Li was not the mastermind and had never spoken to Lai or his right-hand man, Mark Simon, a former US naval intelligence analyst.
The lawyer also argued foreign sanctions were not the necessary consequence of the group’s international lobbying efforts, although SWHK could have contributed to hostile actions from abroad.
The hearing resumes on Tuesday.

data.title: How ‘local’ stars like Bad Bunny show you don’t need to sing in English to be a global hit
data.description:
Labubu dolls knocked against the Chanel purses of club-goers at an after-party at Macau’s MGM Cotai in October, their fiendish smiles mirroring the faces of their owners, who bounced to the beat.
“Lai cai!” the rapper Skai Isyourgod yelled, thumping his chest.
“Lai!” the crowd chanted back.
“Lai cai!” he called again.
“Lai!” the crowd roared.
The hook – “Riches come!” – from Skai’s hit single “Stacks from All Sides” is classic hip-hop fare. Less typical is the artist delivering it.
Skai, whose Chinese stage name is Lan Lao, brought his intoxicating mix of Memphis-style rap with Mandarin, Cantonese and Hakka rhymes to the after-party for American rapper Travis Scott’s Macau concert.

With earworms like “Blueprint Supreme” and “Karma Code”, he has become inescapable in China – sparking social media dance trends and starring in ads for Alipay. But he has also taken off outside the country.
Since releasing his EP in July, Skai’s monthly Spotify streams have surpassed even those of Jay Chou, the long-reigning titan of Mandopop. But unlike Chou, whose popularity was built largely among Chinese-speaking audiences worldwide, Skai is a rare example of Chinese cultural crossover.
He spent November and December on an 11-city North American tour, following dozens of shows in cities that included Sydney, Jakarta and London. His music has found fans with no grasp of Chinese – as one TikTok commenter put it: “Understanding 0%, Vibing 100%.”
Skai’s ascent is more than a breakout success story. It represents a broader shift in the streaming era: the most locally specific music – rooted in accent, place and cultural texture – is increasingly the work that travels the farthest. The trend flips a long-held industry assumption that non-Western artists must sand down their cultural edges to achieve global reach.
For years, Korean megastars like BTS and Blackpink have drawn heavily on Western pop traditions. With Hybe launching the global girl group Katseye and the KPop Demon Hunters soundtrack sung almost entirely in English, there is debate over what “counts” as K-pop.
But now a different logic is gaining momentum: the artists breaking through globally are not diluting their identities, but doubling down on them.
No one illustrates this more clearly than Bad Bunny, who with 19.8 billion plays was Spotify’s most-streamed artist in 2025 – the fourth time he has claimed the top spot. Although he is fluent in English, the Puerto Rican trap star performs only in Spanish and unapologetically opted to stage an extended residency in San Juan rather than tour internationally last summer.
“Gen Z and younger audiences really are looking for and prioritise cultural authenticity,” says Laura Fisher, an entertainment analyst at MIDiA Research.
In her June report, “International Success for Local Language Artists”, Fisher cites the global success of Gaelic-speaking rap act Kneecap, Italian rock band Maneskin and Spanish-Moroccan drill rapper Morad as evidence that language is no longer a limiting factor. Hyperspecificity, she argues, signals freshness and helps artists stand out.

Spanish singer Rosalia is testing this logic with her critically acclaimed album Lux, released in November 2025, which features vocals in 13 languages. “There are visuals of Spain, visuals of what it’s like to be a Catholic,” says Fisher. “It’s opening up that culture to a different audience rather than diluting it.”
There is “a new generation of truly borderless listeners who don’t care about long-held boundaries of language or location”, says James Foley, Spotify’s global editorial strategy lead.
He predicts the trend will accelerate in 2026 with “more Asian artists breaking through globally across a broader mix of styles and sounds” – including Japanese rapper Yuki Chiba and Indonesian indie soul band Thee Marloes.
Skai, 26, transplants the shadowy, lo-fi rhythms of Memphis hip-hop onto the soundscape of southeastern China, weaving in Cantonese opera samples, traditional instruments and philosophical aphorisms.
The video for “Blueprint Supreme” roams through iconic Hong Kong venues including the Happy Valley Racecourse and the Lin Heung teahouse. In the hypnotic track “Nin Jiom”, he raps about Pei Pa Koa – a Chinese cough syrup brand that has recently gained cachet among Western celebrities.

Hong Kong cultural entrepreneur Kevin Poon, who was in the DJ booth that night, says the rise of mumble rap over the past decade – through artists such as Future, Lil Uzi Vert and 21 Savage – has already trained audiences to pay less attention to lyrics.
“You can’t hear what they’re saying anyway,” Poon says. “It doesn’t really matter what language it is, so long as it has a good beat and good rhythm to it.”
Skai, whose real name is Chen Xukai, is from Huizhou in Guangdong province. The city is home to Mount Luofu, a sacred site in Taoism and Buddhism – influences that run strongly through his music.
He studied music performance at a university in Sichuan, then worked briefly at an electricity company and even sold Lao Gan Ma chilli sauce via livestream before committing fully to his rap career. After experiments with emo rap and other styles, he eventually found his sound.
“I was non-stop copying Europe and America,” Skai said in an interview with Chinese media. “No matter how I copied, it didn’t feel right. It wasn’t me.”
He ultimately broke through with what Leung Wai-mun, a linguist and associate professor at Hong Kong Polytechnic University, describes as a “unique southern accent collage”, drawing on pronunciations from Guangxi, Fujian, Hainan and Southeast Asian Chinese communities.
The embrace of linguistic diversity marks a reversal from the early years of communist rule, Leung says, when regional dialects were discouraged to unify the nation and boost literacy. Today, dialects are being rediscovered as important markers of identity, she says. They are “now seen as cultural assets rather than backward”.
Yan Jiayun, social media director for Radii, an online platform that tracks Asian youth culture, says Skai offers something fresh even to Chinese listeners. His flattened, Hakka-inflected Cantonese – a rural cousin to the variety spoken in Hong Kong – inspired Yan to learn more about the Hakka, a Han Chinese subgroup with a distinctive identity, language and culture.
“I felt like I [was] not just watching people rapping,” Yan says. “I [was] also watching a whole kind of fragment of culture that unfolded for me.”
For the overseas Chinese diaspora, Skai’s music plays into a powerful nostalgia factor. Many families left the country before the Communist Party came to power and before Mandarin was adopted as the lingua franca. Hearing an artist perform in something other than Mandarin can feel like a homecoming.
Andrew Chau, a Chinese-American entrepreneur and founder of the tea shop Boba Guys, saw Skai perform in San Francisco in November. The hybrid accent reminded him of the slightly off-kilter intonation of his father, who is from Guangzhou.
Chau was also struck by Skai’s philosophical lyrics, which he says give “words of wisdom toward a life of peace and prosperity”. Drawing on Taoist and Buddhist thought as well as Confucian ideas of filial piety, the music felt, above all, culturally authentic.
Across global music scenes, artists are increasingly finding power in becoming more – not less – specific about where they come from. Chinese artists, Poon notes, are uniquely positioned to benefit given the size of the domestic market and so do not need to worry about taking off globally.

data.title: Taiwan targets Beijing-linked infiltration with push for stricter punishment
data.description:
Taiwan’s legislature is weighing amendments to an anti-infiltration law that would impose a minimum one-year prison term on Beijing-linked influence and infiltration activities, while critics have raised concerns about tighter social controls.
The proposed changes to the Anti-Infiltration Act, under review by the legislature’s Interior Affairs Committee on Monday, come six years after the law took effect.
They also come amid growing concerns within the government that prosecutions have resulted in disproportionately light sentences despite the heavy investigative burden involved in security cases.
Beijing has repeatedly denied the ruling Democratic Progressive Party’s accusations that it has infiltrated the island. It has accused Taipei of intimidating ordinary Taiwanese people and undermining cross-strait exchanges with the measures.
According to official statistics, prosecutors have indicted 127 people under the law since it was enacted in 2020.
Only five have received final guilty verdicts. Average sentences have ranged from three to six months – outcomes that blunt the law’s deterrent effect, according to DPP lawmakers.
Lawmaker Wang Ting-yu, one of 20 sponsors of the amendment, said investigations into infiltration cases were typically cross-border and covert. As a result, they require far more manpower and resources than ordinary criminal cases.
Yet because the law does not set a statutory minimum sentence, judges often resolve cases through summary proceedings, fines in lieu of imprisonment or suspended sentences, according to Wang.
“That gap between enforcement costs and sentencing outcomes undermines deterrence,” he said, arguing that a one-year minimum term would better reflect the gravity of conduct involving hostile forces and their local collaborators.
DPP lawmaker Su Chiao-hui also questioned the effectiveness of the law, noting that “more than 80 per cent of cases investigated since 2023 have not been prosecuted”.
Taiwan’s justice ministry recorded 583 violations of the law between 2021 and 2025. From 2022 onwards, 127 people were indicted, but only five were convicted. In 2023, the average sentence was three months, while it rose to six months in 2025. Under Taiwanese law, sentences of six months or less can be converted into fines.

Lawmakers are also pushing to broaden the scope of regulated conduct during election campaigns.
More than a dozen DPP legislators have proposed extending the law to cover not only officially registered candidates but also “prospective candidates”. They argue that election interference often begins well before formal registration.
Liang Wen-chieh, vice-chairman of Taiwan’s Mainland Affairs Council, which oversees cross-strait policy, told lawmakers on Monday that while the Anti-Infiltration Act was “useful”, it faced structural limits.
One problem, he said, was that the law’s constituent elements were “extremely specific”, allowing “hostile actors” to “adjust their tactics faster than legislation can respond”.
“No matter what rules we set, the other side can find loopholes,” Liang said.
He pointed to cases where conduct fell outside the law simply because it occurred before a candidate formally registered.
A second problem, Liang added, was that the list of offences was too narrow.
He said the current framework focused heavily on election campaigning, political donations and lobbying.
But modern infiltration tactics increasingly involve “spreading disinformation, inciting harassment or intimidation, manipulating disaster-related information, or undermining public trust in government” response efforts, according to Liang.
“The law can only enumerate a limited number of crimes,” he said. “But what the Communist Party can instruct local collaborators to do is virtually unlimited.”
To address those gaps, lawmakers with the independence-leaning DPP have proposed a range of measures. These include regulating “grey-zone” actors engaged in long-term influence operations outside election periods, expanding restrictions on participation in referendums and recall campaigns, and tightening oversight of online platforms.
In a report submitted to the legislature on Monday, the Mainland Affairs Council said it supported strengthening controls on prospective candidates to prevent outside forces from “laying early groundwork through proxies”.
It urged lawmakers to draw on legislative models from elsewhere, including the United States and Australia, when considering reporting or disclosure requirements.


The justice ministry has cautioned that introducing a one-year mandatory minimum sentence could “raise proportionality concerns in less serious cases”, particularly where courts are unable to grant suspended sentences or reduce penalties.
It warned that the change could “conflict with the principle that punishment should be commensurate with culpability”.
The ministry has also expressed reservations about proposals to establish “specialised security courts”. It noted that Taiwan’s judiciary had already adjusted internal court assignment rules to include Anti-Infiltration Act cases within existing security and military case categories.
Meanwhile, Taiwan’s interior ministry has urged lawmakers to clearly define the term “prospective candidate” if it is to be incorporated into criminal provisions.
It warned that vague definitions could “clash with constitutional protections of political participation” and the requirement for clarity in criminal law.
On Monday, Su rejected claims that the amendments amounted to tighter social control.
She said the purpose was to eliminate legal blind spots, not to criminalise ordinary political activity.
“In democratic systems, elections don’t start only after registration,” she said, urging opposition parties not to mischaracterise the revisions.
Supporters say the amendments are increasingly urgent as Beijing intensifies what officials describe as grey-zone pressure, including election interference, disinformation campaigns and psychological coercion.
Opponents, led by the Kuomintang, the island’s main opposition party, and the smaller Taiwan People’s Party, counter that the revisions would “grant the DPP government overly broad powers to criminalise political dissent and restrict cross-strait exchanges”. They also point to the difficulty of “proving a direct link” between local actors and those giving the orders on the mainland.
According to Liang, the value of the law lies in ensuring Taiwan has at least one traceable legal instrument when attempts are made to interfere in its democratic processes.
“The question is not whether the law works, but how to make it keep pace,” he said.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.

data.title: DeepSeek founder’s High-Flyer ranks among China’s top hedge-fund firms in 2025
data.description:
High-Flyer Quant, co-owned by DeepSeek founder and CEO Liang Wenfeng, recorded a return of 56.6 per cent last year to rank second among China’s 10 top-performing large hedge-fund firms.
According to the ranking published on Monday by data provider Shenzhen PaiPaiWang Investment & Management, High-Flyer only trailed behind Lingjun Investment, which posted a return of 73.5 per cent in 2025.
All the top performers were quantitative hedge funds, underscoring the popularity in China of using complex quantitative models to detect investment opportunities.
Quant funds use mathematical and statistical techniques, together with automated algorithms and advanced quantitative models, to make investment decisions and execute trades.
Hangzhou-based High-Flyer’s stellar 2025 performance – among Chinese hedge funds with more than 10 billion yuan (US$1.43 billion) in managed assets – was based on the 10 index funds it operated as Ningbo High-Flyer Quant Investment Management Partnership.
Still, some smaller funds achieved higher returns last year, according to the Shenzhen PaiPaiWang report. Those included Shanghai-based Hengshui Asset Management, with a 239 per cent return, and Zhihe Asset Management, with a 108 per cent annual yield.
High-Flyer, which managed assets of between 70 billion yuan and 80 billion yuan as of the third quarter of 2025, ranked as one of the biggest large hedge-fund firms in China, according to QIML, a Chinese blogger with a focus on quantitative trading and machine learning.

The 2025 returns recorded by the best large hedge-fund firms outperformed the mainland’s stock markets, as the benchmark Shanghai Composite Index, Shenzhen Composite Index and CSI 300 Index only gained 18, 30 and 18 per cent, respectively.
Founded in 2015, privately held High-Flyer launched for real-time trading on October 21, 2016, using advanced graphics processing units for calculation, according to its website. Before that, funds mainly relied on traditional machine-learning algorithms, while the model calculation mainly relied on personal computer central processing units.
With hefty resources at his disposal, Liang established DeepSeek – previously the artificial intelligence lab of High-Flyer – as a separate company in July 2023.
Liang earlier said that DeepSeek was in no rush to get new investors, partly because of sufficient funding from High-Flyer.
High-Flyer, for example, had subscribed to a total of 82,244 shares of Moore Threads Technology before the chip designer went public in Shanghai last month. High-Flyer appeared to gain a hefty profit from that outlay, as Moore Threads’ shares rose more than five times in its first two trading days after raising about 8 billion yuan from its initial public offering.

data.title: Europe’s real threat is its own inner ‘demon’, not Russia: former Chinese envoy
data.description:
Europe made a “misjudgment” in categorising Russia as its top security threat and instead the continent’s real danger lay in Europeans’ inner “demon”, according to China’s longest-serving ambassador to the US.
Cui Tiankai, a former Chinese foreign vice-minister, also claimed Nato had outlived its relevance and argued that the transatlantic security alliance no longer served the long-term interests of its members.
Cui’s comments came during a discussion examining US relations with its allies under the Donald Trump administration at the World Peace Forum, organised by Tsinghua University’s Institute of International Relations in Beijing on Sunday.
The event took place amid widespread speculation about Nato’s role and future as the US president ramps up talk of taking over Greenland, an autonomous territory of Denmark, an American ally.

Questions have intensified since the US military operation this month in Venezuela that culminated in the abduction of the South American country’s leader, Nicolas Maduro, and his wife. The two now face narcoterrorism charges in New York.
Following Trump’s remarks last week that the US was “going to do something on Greenland, whether they like it or not”, Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen on Sunday described the situation as a “fateful moment” for her country and Nato.
According to Cui, aside from examining where ties stand between the US and its European allies, it was worth considering whether Nato was necessary at all.
With the Cold War long ended and the Soviet-led Warsaw Pact dissolved, Cui said Nato should have disbanded more than three decades ago.
A military bloc could not resolve major contemporary global challenges, such as supply chain resilience and climate change, nor emerging technology issues like artificial intelligence, he added.
Cui said Nato’s preservation had run counter to the arc of history and would “inevitably” breed numerous internal divisions, contradictions and problems.
“Fundamentally, such an alliance system … is no longer in line with the pattern of global development,” the former diplomat added.
Cui said Nato could not meet the aspirations of the international community, particularly the Global South and that “it may not even serve the long-term interests of its own member states”.


European capitals’ calls for continued American security guarantees, he continued, raised the question: what exactly constitutes a threat to Europe?
“At least a significant portion of Europeans regard Russia as their greatest security threat,” Cui said. “I believe that is a misjudgment. China is certainly even less of [a threat].”
He argued that the greatest threat to Europe’s security was “the ‘demon’ in their own minds”, saying they approached 21st-century security with world views dating back centuries that rendered them unable to truly “gain” or “feel” a sense of security.
In a veiled swipe at countries such as Britain, France and Germany, Cui accused some European Nato members of extending their military reach to Asia, including sending warships to the South China Sea and the Taiwan Strait, which he said was “unwise”.
“They need to understand that since the end of World War II, this alliance system of theirs … has never won in Asia,” he added.
“I have told Europeans that should Nato extend its reach into Asia it would genuinely mark the beginning of your decline.”
At the same event, Yan Xuetong, honorary dean of Tsinghua’s Institute of International Relations, also criticised European countries for seeking to expand their military presence in East Asia before ensuring security within their own region, citing the current war in Ukraine and the Kosovo armed conflict of 1998-1999.
“Europe is so proud as if it represents the most advanced [direction] of human civilisation,” Yan said. “[But] it cannot even guarantee peace in its own home.”
“I think Europeans may now need to address a mindset problem.”
